steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks how Tsai et al. (Text 2) would respond to Madhusudhan et al.'s claim in Text 1. Text 1 says K2-18 b sits at about 1 AU, right on the inner edge of the HZ. Text 2 says hydrogen-rich atmospheres cause elevated surface temperatures, pushing the true inner HZ edge out to 3.85 AU. A planet at 1 AU is therefore well inside the new inner edge — too hot to keep liquid water.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "By observing that unlike the hycean candidate Madhusudhan et al. discovered, most other types of planets with hydrogen-rich atmospheres are likely located within the HZ.": Tsai's argument applies specifically to hydrogen-rich atmospheres, which is exactly the type K2-18 b has — there is no contrast between K2-18 b and other hydrogen-rich planets. ✗
  • "By maintaining that Madhusudhan et al. relied on a model whose estimates of surface temperatures on hycean candidates are likely too high.": Tsai argues temperatures are too low in the original model, not too high. Tsai's whole point is that hydrogen-rich atmospheres admit wavelengths that raise surface temperatures above what earlier models predicted. ✗
  • "By arguing that K2-18 b and other hycean candidates are unlikely to support life because these planets are located too far from the stars they orbit.": Tsai says K2-18 b is too close to its star (inside the HZ), not too far. ✗
  • "By stating that the chemical composition of the atmosphere of the hycean candidate Madhusudhan et al. identified suggests that this planet's surface is unlikely to harbor liquid water.": Tsai's key finding is that the hydrogen-rich atmosphere itself drives temperatures too high to retain liquid water. This accurately captures Tsai's challenge to Madhusudhan et al. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
